ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Chinese-style crispy battered chicken pieces; closest match is generic fried chicken food product (FOODON_03302051).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers fried chicken meat generically; no specific code for Chinese-style battered crispy chicken pieces exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for crispy fried chicken pieces; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 side portion of crispy chicken pieces
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side serving of crispy fried chicken pieces is approximately 150 g, equivalent to 4–6 bite-sized pieces as served at a hawker stall or casual dining outlet in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (SR Legacy ID 05062)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input nutrient values (energy 300 kcal, protein 18.67 g, fat 20.0 g, carbohydrate 12.0 g per 100 g) are broadly consistent with USDA SR Legacy entry for batter-fried chicken (approximately 277–320 kcal, 17–20 g protein, 17–21 g fat, 10–14 g carbohydrate per 100 g). Values retained from input with USDA SR Legacy used as the approved anchor source. Singapore HPB Foodo database does not carry a direct entry for this specific preparation; USDA SR Legacy is the closest approved source. Sodium 433 mg/100 g is higher than USDA baseline (~280 mg) and reflects the soy-sauce marinade typical of Chinese-Singaporean preparation; value retained from input with this caveat noted.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken / wheat-flour batter coating
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese-style crispy fried chicken pieces. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being a wheat-flour and cornstarch batter coating, which is analogous to battered fried foods with GI values in the 60–70 range as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 18.0) / 100 = 11.7 (150 g serving × 12.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 18.0 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 433 mg per 100 g; low dietary fibre 0.67 g per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 75 g per meal, request reduced soy sauce in the marinade, and avoid additional dipping sauces to keep cumulative sodium intake within the 2000 mg daily target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate 12 g per 100 g from wheat-flour batter; medium GI 65; high fat 20 g per 100 g increases caloric density
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume a small portion (75–100 g) occasionally, pairing it with low-GI non-starchy vegetables to moderate the postprandial glucose response, while monitoring total energy intake given the high fat content from deep-frying.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.33 g per 100 g; trans fat 0.13 g per 100 g; cholesterol 66.67 mg per 100 g; high sodium 433 mg per 100 g; deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of this deep-fried item due to its combined saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium load; if consumed, limit to a single small portion (≤75 g) infrequently and prefer oven-baked or air-fried alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs that promote joint inflammation; saturated fat 5.33 g per 100 g at pro-inflammatory threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparation is a hard disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis management due to AGE formation; individuals with RA should avoid this dish or request oven-baked or air-fried preparation to substantially reduce AGE content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Fried Chicken Pieces (香酥鸡块, Xiāng Sū Jī Kuài) is a popular Chinese-Singaporean dish consisting of bite-sized chicken pieces coated in a seasoned wheat-flour and cornstarch batter, then deep-fried until golden and crunchy. The exterior is characteristically light and shatteringly crisp, while the interior remains juicy and tender. The chicken is typically marinated in soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and salt before battering, giving it a savoury umami flavour profile with subtle aromatic notes. It is commonly served as a side dish, snack, or topping alongside noodle dishes, rice noodles, or skewers at hawker centres and casual Chinese-Singaporean eateries such as Miss Tang's. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving provides approximately 300 kcal, 18.7 g protein, 20 g fat (5.3 g saturated), and 12 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 433 mg per 100 g due to the soy-sauce marinade. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) based on the wheat-flour batter coating. Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, saturated fat, and trans fat content, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its medium GI and high caloric density from deep-frying. It is not suitable for individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation during deep-frying.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crispy chicken pieces?
Can diabetics eat crispy fried chicken?
Is 香酥鸡块 suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of fried chicken?

meal_context: Part of a mixed rice or noodle meal

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are broadly consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for batter-fried chicken but originate from a GrabFood delivery platform listing (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). The sodium value (433 mg/100 g) is higher than the USDA baseline and reflects the Chinese-Singaporean soy-sauce marinade preparation. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists for this specific dish. USDA SR Legacy is used as the approved anchor source with adaptation noted. GI is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Chinese-Singaporean crispy fried chicken preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database at the level of this specific dish as of 2024. 2. vitamin_c_mg set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy for batter-fried chicken; no vitamin C-contributing ingredients are present in the standard preparation. 3. Sodium value of 433 mg/100 g is higher than the USDA SR Legacy baseline (~280 mg/100 g) and reflects the soy-sauce and salt marinade typical of Chinese-Singaporean preparation; this value is retained from the input source with this caveat. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour and cornstarch batter coating; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Halal flag set to true as chicken is the primary protein and no pork or alcohol ingredients are indicated in the standard preparation; this should be verified against the specific vendor's certification. 6. Input file name contains a typographical variant 'Chrispy' (vs. canonical 'Crispy'); both forms are captured in aliases.
